content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "\u7eb8\u5ac1\u88632\u5958\u94c3\u6751 - \u5bc6\u5ba4\u9003\u8131\u7c7b\u6050\u6016\u89e3\u8c1c\u6e38\u620f",
  "game_name": "Bell Village Echoes",
  "genre": "Puzzle, Escape Room, Light Horror",
  "core_mechanics": "The game operates as a first-person, fixed-viewpoint escape room. Players navigate between predefined scenes, each representing a different area or perspective within a room. \n\n**Player Actions:**\n1.  **Scene Navigation:** Players can switch between connected scenes/viewpoints using designated keyboard keys.\n2.  **Object Selection/Highlighting:** Within a scene, players can cycle through 'hotspots' (interactive objects or areas) using keyboard keys. The currently selected hotspot receives visual feedback (e.g., a glowing outline).\n3.  **Interaction:** Pressing an 'interact' key on a selected hotspot triggers an action. This can include:\n    *   **Examine:** Displaying a close-up image or text description of the object.\n    *   **Pick Up:** Adding a collectable item to the player's inventory.\n    *   **Activate Puzzle:** Opening a dedicated full-screen puzzle interface (e.g., a numpad, a slider puzzle).\n    *   **Use Inventory Item:** Attempting to combine a currently active inventory item with the selected hotspot.\n4.  **Inventory Management:** Players can open/close an inventory overlay to view collected items. While the inventory is open, they can select an item, making it 'active' for use on environmental hotspots.\n5.  **Puzzle Solving:** Interacting with specific puzzle interfaces requires direct input (e.g., number keys for codes, arrow keys for manipulating puzzle elements).\n\n**Rules and Systems:**\n*   **Scene Graph:** Scenes are interconnected, forming a graph structure. Navigation only allows movement to adjacent scenes.\n*   **Object States:** Each interactive object maintains a state (e.g., 'locked', 'unlocked', 'examined', 'hasItem', 'puzzleSolved'). Actions are conditional on these states.\n*   **Inventory System:** Stores collected `Item` objects. Items have properties like `id`, `name`, `description`, `sprite`. Items can be 'used' on specific `Hotspot` objects to trigger events or solve puzzles.\n*   **Puzzle Logic:** Each puzzle has a set of predefined conditions for completion. Successful completion often changes object states and may unlock new areas or items.\n*   **Hint System:** Players can request hints, which provide a textual clue. Using a hint incurs a score penalty.\n*   **Time Limit:** Each level has a time limit. Exceeding it results in a 'Game Over'.\n\n**State Tracking:**\n*   `currentSceneId`: ID of the scene currently displayed.\n*   `inventory`: An array of `Item` objects currently held by the player.\n*   `hotspotStates`: A dictionary mapping hotspot IDs to their current state flags (e.g., `{ 'door_handle': 'unlocked', 'old_book': 'examined' }`).\n*   `activePuzzleId`: ID of the puzzle currently open, or `null`.\n*   `activeInventoryItemId`: ID of the inventory item currently selected for use.\n*   `score`: Player's current cumulative score.\n*   `levelTimeRemaining`: Time left for the current level.\n*   `hintsUsedThisLevel`: Counter for hints taken in the current level.\n*   `currentLevel`: Integer representing the current level number.",
  "visual_elements": "All visual elements will be rendered using p5.js, with Matter.js potentially used for specific, simple physics-based puzzles if included.\n\n**Game Objects & Visual Representations:**\n1.  **Backgrounds/Scenes:** Static, high-resolution image files (PNG/JPG) for each unique viewpoint. These will be loaded once per level.\n2.  **Interactive Objects (Hotspots):** Defined as regions (rectangles, polygons) on the background images. When selected, they will display a distinct visual highlight, such as a pulsating glow animation or a bright outline (e.g., yellow, blue).\n3.  **Collectable Items:** Small sprite images (PNG) that appear within scenes. Upon interaction and collection, they animate by scaling down and moving towards the inventory area.\n4.  **Inventory Display:** A UI overlay, appearing when the inventory is opened (Z key). It consists of a row of rectangular 'slots', each displaying the sprite of a collected item. The currently selected item will have a distinct border (e.g., green).\n5.  **Puzzle Interfaces:** Dedicated full-screen modal overlays that appear when a puzzle is activated. Each puzzle will have its unique visual layout using p5.js shapes, text, and potentially small sprites (e.g., a numpad with clickable buttons, a slider puzzle with draggable tiles).\n6.  **UI Elements (Always Visible):**\n    *   **Score Display:** Text in the top-right corner.\n    *   **Level Indicator:** Text in the top-left corner.\n    *   **Time Remaining:** Text (e.g., 'TIMER: MM:SS') in the top-center.\n    *   **Hint Button (Optional/Visual Cue):** A small icon or text in a corner that subtly changes when the Shift key is held.\n\n**Animations & Visual Effects:**\n*   **Hotspot Highlight:** Pulsating outline or glow effect around the selected interactive area.\n*   **Item Collection:** Smooth scaling down animation combined with a translation animation to the inventory slot.\n*   **Scene Transition:** A brief fade-to-black/fade-in effect, or a horizontal slide animation when moving between scenes.\n*   **Successful Interaction:** A brief 'sparkle' or 'glint' effect over the successfully interacted object, often accompanied by a positive sound.\n*   **Unsuccessful Interaction:** A subtle 'red tint' flash on the object, or a 'wiggle' animation, accompanied by a negative sound.\n*   **Puzzle Feedback:** Green checkmark for correct input, red cross for incorrect input, or elements visually reacting to player input.\n*   **Light Horror Effects (Subtle):** Occasional, brief screen distortions, subtle visual glitches (e.g., static noise overlay), or fleeting, barely perceptible ghostly outlines appearing and disappearing to maintain an unsettling atmosphere without overt jump scares. These would be rare and context-specific.",
  "control_scheme": "The control scheme prioritizes keyboard interactions for all gameplay actions, ensuring clear mapping and responsiveness.\n\n*   **ArrowLeft / ArrowRight:** Navigate between connected scenes/viewpoints. (e.g., move to the scene on the left/right).\n*   **ArrowUp / ArrowDown:** Cycle through interactive hotspots within the current scene. ArrowUp moves to the previous hotspot in a defined list, ArrowDown to the next. The currently selected hotspot will be highlighted.\n*   **Space:** Perform the primary action on the currently selected hotspot or puzzle element.\n    *   If a hotspot is selected: Interact, examine, pick up item, or activate a puzzle.\n    *   If an inventory item is active: Attempt to use the active inventory item on the selected hotspot.\n    *   If a puzzle interface is open: Confirm input, interact with a puzzle element.\n    *   During level transition: Advance to the next level.\n*   **Z:** Toggle Inventory overlay (open/close). If an item is selected in inventory, closes inventory and makes that item 'active' for use on the environment.\n*   **A / D (when Inventory is open):** Navigate through items in the inventory. 'A' selects the previous item, 'D' selects the next item. The selected item will be visually highlighted.\n*   **Shift (Hold):** Activate the hint system. Holding Shift for a brief moment will display a hint for the current puzzle or objective, deducting points.\n*   **Escape:** Pause/Unpause the game when in PLAYING state. Close current puzzle interface or inventory if open, returning to PLAYING state.",
  "game_states": "The game flow is managed through distinct states, ensuring clear transitions and modular logic.\n\n1.  **LOADING:** \n    *   **Description:** Initial state where all game assets (images, sounds, puzzle data) are loaded.\n    *   **Triggers:** Game starts.\n    *   **Transitions:** -> MENU (Once all essential assets are loaded).\n2.  **MENU:**\n    *   **Description:** Displays the main title, 'Start Game', 'High Scores', and 'Options' selections.\n    *   **Triggers:** From LOADING state; From WIN_SCREEN or GAME_OVER screen after player input.\n    *   **Transitions:** -> PLAYING (When 'Start Game' is selected); -> HIGH_SCORES (When 'High Scores' is selected).\n3.  **PLAYING:**\n    *   **Description:** The core gameplay loop. Player navigates scenes, interacts with objects, solves puzzles.\n    *   **Triggers:** From MENU state (Start Game); From PAUSED state (Resume).\n    *   **Transitions:** \n        *   -> PAUSED (Escape key).\n        *   -> INVENTORY_OPEN (Z key).\n        *   -> PUZZLE_ACTIVE (Space key on a puzzle hotspot).\n        *   -> LEVEL_COMPLETE_SCREEN (Level goal achieved).\n        *   -> GAME_OVER (Time limit reached or critical failure).\n4.  **INVENTORY_OPEN:**\n    *   **Description:** Inventory overlay is visible. Player can select items.\n    *   **Triggers:** From PLAYING state (Z key).\n    *   **Transitions:** -> PLAYING (Z key again, or successful use of an item on a hotspot).\n5.  **PUZZLE_ACTIVE:**\n    *   **Description:** A specific puzzle interface (e.g., numpad, slider) is displayed, blocking scene interaction.\n    *   **Triggers:** From PLAYING state (Space key on a puzzle hotspot).\n    *   **Transitions:** -> PLAYING (Puzzle solved, or puzzle interface manually closed).\n6.  **PAUSED:**\n    *   **Description:** Game execution is halted. Pause menu displayed.\n    *   **Triggers:** From PLAYING state (Escape key).\n    *   **Transitions:** -> PLAYING (Escape key again or 'Resume' selected); -> MENU (On 'Quit to Menu' selection).\n7.  **LEVEL_COMPLETE_SCREEN:**\n    *   **Description:** Displays level-end summary (score, bonuses, total). Awaits player to advance.\n    *   **Triggers:** From PLAYING state (Final puzzle of level solved).\n    *   **Transitions:** -> PLAYING (Space key to start next level); -> WIN_SCREEN (If last level completed).\n8.  **GAME_OVER:**\n    *   **Description:** Displayed when a losing condition is met (e.g., time runs out). Shows final score.\n    *   **Triggers:** From PLAYING state (Time runs out).\n    *   **Transitions:** -> MENU (Player input 'Return to Menu').\n9.  **WIN_SCREEN:**\n    *   **Description:** Displayed when all levels are successfully completed. Shows final cumulative score.\n    *   **Triggers:** From LEVEL_COMPLETE_SCREEN (After completing the final level).\n    *   **Transitions:** -> MENU (Player input 'Return to Menu').",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins by successfully completing all puzzles across all defined levels.\n\n*   **Level Win:** Solving the final, primary puzzle of the current level. This triggers a transition to the `LEVEL_COMPLETE_SCREEN`.\n*   **Game Win:** Solving the final, primary puzzle of the very last level. This triggers the `WIN_SCREEN`.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player loses under the following conditions:\n\n*   **Time Limit Exceeded:** Each level has a strict time limit. If the `levelTimeRemaining` reaches zero before the primary puzzle of the level is solved, the game transitions to the `GAME_OVER` screen.\n*   (Simplified from original horror concept): No critical 'death' scenarios from specific choices or enemies. Failures in puzzles typically allow retry without immediate game over, but may incur minor penalties (e.g., score deduction for too many attempts on a complex mechanism).",
  "scoring_system": "A robust scoring system encourages efficient and clever puzzle solving, with a high score tracker.\n\n*   **Point Awards:**\n    *   **+200 points:** Per collectable item found and added to inventory.\n    *   **+500 points:** Per minor puzzle solved (e.g., finding a hidden clue, solving a simple sequence).\n    *   **+1000 points:** Per major puzzle solved (e.g., combination lock, multi-step riddle).\n*   **Point Deductions:**\n    *   **-100 points:** Per hint used via the Shift key.\n*   **Bonus Points (Per Level Completion):**\n    *   **Time Bonus:** `(Remaining Time in Seconds * 10)` points awarded at the end of each level.\n    *   **'No Hint' Bonus:** `+1000 points` if a level is completed without using any hints.\n    *   **'Speed Run' Bonus:** `+500 points` if a level is completed within 60% of the allocated time limit.\n*   **High Score System:** The game will track the top 5 highest cumulative scores across all levels, stored locally using `localStorage`.\n*   **Score Display:** The current score will always be displayed prominently in the **top-right corner** of the screen in the format `SCORE: XXXXXX` (6-digit, zero-padded integer).\n*   **Score Impact:** The score primarily serves as a metric for player skill and contributes to the high score board. It does not directly affect gameplay difficulty or unlock game content, keeping the focus on puzzle solving.",
  "level_system": "The game features 3 distinct levels, each progressively increasing in complexity, number of interactive elements, and challenge.\n\n1.  **Level 1: The Dilapidated Shrine Entrance**\n    *   **Setting:** Players begin in a dimly lit, weathered outdoor entrance area of an ancient shrine. Visuals include stone steps, overgrown foliage, and a locked main door.\n    *   **Goal:** Gain entry to the inner sanctum by finding a hidden key.\n    *   **Puzzles:** \n        *   **Hidden Object:** Find a small, obscured symbol on a stone carving.\n        *   **Simple Combination:** Arrange three distinct stone tablets in a specific order based on visual cues.\n        *   **Cipher Decryption:** Use a simple substitution cipher to unlock a small wooden box.\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Introductory level. Clues are relatively direct and confined to a small number of interactive objects. The scene graph is linear (e.g., Entrance -> Small Courtyard -> Main Door View).\n    *   **Time Limit:** 10 minutes.\n2.  **Level 2: The Inner Sanctum**\n    *   **Setting:** Upon entering, players find themselves in a cluttered, darker inner room. Altar, dusty scrolls, and unsettling effigies create a more foreboding atmosphere.\n    *   **Goal:** Discover a specific ritual item and locate fragments of a torn ancient scroll.\n    *   **Puzzles:** \n        *   **Multi-step Item Usage:** Find a specific liquid, then use it on a sealed scroll to reveal text, then use a tool to extract a key from a hidden compartment.\n        *   **Slider Puzzle:** A 3x3 or 4x4 image slider puzzle on an ancient chest.\n        *   **Symbol Matching:** Match cryptic symbols found across different parts of the room to unlock a cabinet.\n        *   **Subtle Sound Sequence:** A puzzle requiring the player to 'interact' with objects in a specific audio/visual sequence.\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** Moderate. More interactive objects, some 'red herring' items. Clues are less obvious and require combining information from different viewpoints. The scene graph becomes slightly branched.\n    *   **Time Limit:** 12 minutes.\n3.  **Level 3: The Crypt of Echoes**\n    *   **Setting:** A claustrophobic, underground crypt, visually and acoustically the most unsettling. Dark, damp, with eerie echoes and ancestral relics. Subtle, infrequent visual glitches or fleeting apparitions might occur.\n    *   **Goal:** Complete a final ritual using collected items and scroll fragments to 'seal' the haunting presence.\n    *   **Puzzles:** \n        *   **Cryptic Riddle:** Decipher a complex text-based riddle to determine an item's correct placement.\n        *   **Weight Balance Logic:** A puzzle requiring precise placement of weights on a scale using collected items to balance it.\n        *   **Multi-stage Mechanism:** Activating a series of levers or gears in a precise, timed sequence to open a final chamber.\n        *   **Scroll Reconstruction:** Piecing together the torn scroll fragments to reveal a final incantation or code.\n        *   **Final Ritual:** A complex interaction puzzle requiring the use of multiple specific items in a precise order on a central altar.\n    *   **Difficulty Progression:** High. Puzzles are deeply interconnected and abstract. Many potential interactions, but only a few are relevant. Hints might only provide general direction. The scene graph can be more complex, with hidden pathways.\n    *   **Time Limit:** 15 minutes.\n\n**Level Advancement:** The player automatically advances to the next level (or `WIN_SCREEN` for the last level) immediately after successfully solving the final primary puzzle of the current level. This triggers the `LEVEL_COMPLETE_SCREEN` display.\n\n**Level Transition Display:** Upon completing a level, the `LEVEL_COMPLETE_SCREEN` will display:\n*   \"LEVEL COMPLETE!\" (or \"GAME COMPLETE!\" for the final level) prominently.\n*   Summary of points gained in the level: \"LEVEL SCORE: XXXXXX\", \"TIME BONUS: XXXXXX\", \"NO HINT BONUS: XXXXXX\", \"SPEED RUN BONUS: XXXXXX\".\n*   \"TOTAL SCORE: XXXXXX\".\n*   \"PRESS SPACE FOR NEXT LEVEL\" (or \"PRESS SPACE TO RETURN TO MENU\" for game completion).\n\n**Level Indicator:** A constant level indicator `LEVEL: X` (e.g., `LEVEL: 1`) will be displayed in the **top-left corner** of the screen during gameplay.",
  "simplifications": "The following features from the original detailed game concept (implied by genre, as full details were not provided) are simplified for an HTML5 implementation with p5.js/Matter.js:\n\n*   **Horror Intensity:** Reduced from a potentially intense horror experience to 'light horror.' This means focusing on atmospheric tension through visuals (dark, desaturated palettes, subtle distortions) and audio (eerie ambient sounds, unsettling stingers for failed attempts or reveals), rather than frequent, explicit jump scares, gore, or direct threats to the player avatar. The 'paper bride' narrative is abstracted to visual motifs.\n*   **Complex Narrative & Character Development:** The deep lore, complex character backstories, and intricate branching dialogue typical of some escape room or adventure games are abstracted. The focus is purely on immediate puzzle-solving objectives within each level's context (e.g., 'find the key', 'perform the ritual'). Story elements are conveyed minimally through environmental details or simple riddle texts.\n*   **Mobile-Specific Interactions:** All touch-based gestures (swipes, multi-touch, drag-and-drop for inventory) are replaced with a keyboard-centric control scheme. Inventory management and object interaction are driven by explicit key presses, providing clear visual feedback for selection and action.\n*   **Physics Engine Usage (Matter.js):** While Matter.js is included in the potential toolset, it is largely simplified or omitted. Most puzzles are logic-based, inventory-driven, or spatial. Matter.js would only be employed for very specific, simple physics puzzles (e.g., balancing objects, dropping a single item to break something) if designed, otherwise, p5.js will handle all rendering and interaction logic. This avoids complex physics simulations that might be resource-intensive or unnecessary for the core gameplay loop.\n*   **Visual Fidelity & Animations:** Graphics are primarily static 2D background images with overlaid 2D sprites for interactive items and UI elements. Animations are limited to simple transitions (fades, slides), scaling, pulsing highlights, and brief visual effects (sparkles, flashes) rather than complex character animations or 3D rendering.\n*   **Multiplayer Features:** The game is explicitly single-player. No network code or multiplayer components are included."
}
</task>

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>

Bell Village Echoes

Escape the haunted Bell Village shrine by solving puzzles across three increasingly challenging levels. Navigate between viewpoints, examine objects, collect items, and use your wits to unlock doors, decipher codes, and complete ancient rituals. Time is limited - solve all puzzles before the clock runs out to escape successfully. Each level grows darker and more complex as you descend into the Crypt of Echoes.
